items.prompt: Mathematically, a relation R from a set A to a set B is defined to be:
items.answer: an arbitrary subset of A x B

expected.correct: an arbitrary subset of A x B
options: an arbitrary subset of A x B
a recognisable real-world link between A and B
the whole Cartesian product A x B only
a function from A to B

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.1 Definition of a relation

items.prompt: In the set A = {1, 2, 3, 4}, the relation R = {(a, b) : a - b = 10} is the empty relation. How many ordered pairs does R contain?
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.2 Empty relation

items.prompt: A relation R in a set A in which no element of A is related to any element of A is called:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.2 Empty relation

items.prompt: A relation R in a set A in which each element of A is related to every element of A (so R = A x A) is called:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.2 Universal relation

items.prompt: For a relation R in a set A to be reflexive, which pairs must belong to R?
items.answer: (a, a) for every a in A

expected.correct: (a, a) for every a in A
options: (a, a) for every a in A
(a, a) for at least one a in A
(a, b) and (b, a) for all a, b

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.2 Reflexive relation

items.prompt: A relation R is symmetric if, whenever (a1, a2) is in R, which pair must also be in R?
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.2 Symmetric relation

items.prompt: A relation R is transitive if, whenever (a1, a2) and (a2, a3) are in R, which pair must also be in R?
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.2 Transitive relation

items.prompt: A relation R in a set A is an equivalence relation if and only if it is:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.2 Equivalence relation

items.prompt: Show that R = {(a, b) : 2 divides a - b} on the set Z of integers is an equivalence relation.

expected.rubric: R is reflexive since 2 divides a - a = 0 for all a. R is symmetric since if 2 divides a - b then 2 divides b - a. R is transitive since if 2 divides a - b and 2 divides b - c, then a - c = (a - b) + (b - c) is even, so 2 divides a - c. Being reflexive, symmetric and transitive, R is an equivalence relation.
markPoints: Reflexive: 2 divides a - a for all a
Symmetric: 2 divides a - b implies 2 divides b - a
Transitive: a - c = (a - b) + (b - c) is even, so 2 divides a - c; hence equivalence relation

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Example 5

items.prompt: For the relation R = {(a, b) : 2 divides a - b} on the integers Z, how many distinct equivalence classes are there?
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.2 Equivalence classes [0] and [1]

items.prompt: Which function from R to R is one-one (injective)?
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Examples 9 and 11 (one-one test)

items.prompt: Show that f : R -> R given by f(x) = 2x is one-one.

expected.rubric: Assume f(x1) = f(x2). Then 2x1 = 2x2, which gives x1 = x2. Since equal images force equal inputs, distinct elements have distinct images, so f is one-one (injective).

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Example 9

items.prompt: The function f : N -> N given by f(x) = 2x is NOT onto. Which element of the co-domain N is not the image of any element?
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Example 8

items.prompt: A function f : X -> Y is onto if and only if:
items.answer: the range of f equals Y

expected.correct: the range of f equals Y
options: the range of f equals Y
X and Y have the same number of elements

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.3 Remark: onto iff Range of f = Y

items.prompt: A function is bijective if and only if it is:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.3 Bijective
Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Example 15

items.prompt: For two functions f and g, the composition gof is defined by:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.4 Definition 8 (composition)

items.prompt: f : N -> Y given by f(x) = 4x + 3 is invertible with inverse g(y) = (y - 3)/4. Find g(11).
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Example 17

items.prompt: A function f : X -> Y is invertible if there exists g : Y -> X such that:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.4 Definition 9 (invertible)

items.prompt: A function f is invertible if and only if it is:
items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.4 (invertible iff bijective)

items.prompt: Explain how the result 'f is invertible if and only if f is one-one and onto' lets you prove a function invertible without constructing its inverse.

expected.rubric: Since invertibility is equivalent to being one-one and onto (bijective), it suffices to prove f is injective and surjective. If both hold, the theorem guarantees an inverse exists, so there is no need to write the inverse explicitly - useful when the actual inverse is hard to determine.
markPoints: Invertible is equivalent to one-one and onto (bijective)
The theorem then guarantees the inverse exists, without constructing it

items.srcLabel: Authored practice (Opus) - mathematics-ch01 Sec 1.4 (use of invertible iff bijective)
